16.15: And she was immersed and the children of her house and she was begging of us and saying: “If you are truly confident that I have believed in Our Lord, come lodge yourselves at my house”, and she urged us much.
16.16: And it was that as we were going to the house of prayer, there met with us one girl who had a spirit of divination upon her and she was making a great business for her masters in the divination that she had been practicing.
16.17: She was coming after Paulus and after us, crying and saying, “These men are Servants of the highest God, and they are evangelizing to you the way of life.”
16.18: And thus she was doing for many days, and Paulus was angered and he said to that spirit, “I command you in the name of Yeshua The Messiah to come out of her”, and at that moment, it came out.
16.19: When her masters saw that the hope of their business had departed from her, they seized Paulus and Shila, and they dragged and brought them to the marketplace.
16.20: And they presented them to the Magistrate and to the Leaders of the city and they were saying, “These men are troubling our city because they are Jews.”
16.21: “And they are preaching to us customs which are not allowable for us to receive and do, because we are Romans.”
16.22: And great crowds were gathered against them and then the Magistrates tore their garments and commanded to scourge them.
16.23: And when they had scourged them much, they cast them into the prison and commanded the Keeper of the prison to keep them securely.
16.24: But when he had received this order, he brought them and shut them up in the inner room of the prison and bound their feet in the stocks.
16.25: In the middle of the night, Paulus and Shila were praying and singing to God and the prisoners were listening to them.
16.26: And suddenly there was a great earthquake and the foundation of the prison was shaken, and immediately all the doors were opened and their chains were all released.
16.27: And when the Keeper of the prison was awakened and he saw that the doors of the prison were open, he took a sword and sought to kill himself because he thought that the prisoners had escaped.
16.28: And Paulus called him in a loud voice and he said to him, “Do no harm to yourself, because we are all here.”
16.29: And he lit a lamp and he leaped and entered, trembling, and he fell before the feet of Paulus and of Shila,
16.30: And he brought them outside and he said to them, “Sirs, what must I do so that I may live?”
16.31: And they said to him, “Trust in our Lord Yeshua The Messiah, and you shall live, you and your household.”
16.32: And they spoke with him the word of THE LORD JEHOVAH, and with all the people of his household.
16.33: And at that hour of the night, he took and washed them of their wounds and immediately he was immersed and all the people of his household.
16.34: And he led and brought them to his house and he set a table for them, and he rejoiced and the people of his house in the faith of God.
16.35: And when it was dawn, the Magistrates sent the Rod Bearers to say to The Warden of the prison, “Release these men.”
16.36: And when The Warden of the prison heard, he entered and he spoke this message to Paulus: “The Magistrates have sent so that you should be released, and now depart; go in peace.”
16.37: Paulus said to him, “They scourged us without an offense, publicly before the world, all of us being Roman men, and they cast us into prison, and now they are releasing us secretly? No, but those men should come and release us.”
16.38: And the Rod Bearers went and they told the Magistrates these words that were spoken to them, and when they heard that they were Romans, they were afraid.
16.39: And they came to them and they begged them to go out and depart from the city.
16.40: And when they went out from the prison they returned to Lydia, and there they saw the brethren and comforted them, and they left.

17.1: They passed unto the cities Amphipolis and Apollonia and they came to Thessaloniqa where there was a synagogue of the Jews.
17.2: And Paulus entered as he was accustomed with them, and on the third Sabbath he spoke with them from the Scripture,
17.3: As he was expounding and showing: “The Messiah was going to suffer and rise from the grave, and he is Yeshua The Messiah, this one whom I proclaim to you.”
17.4: And some of them believed and joined Paulus and Shila, and many of those Greeks who were worshipers of God, even notable women, not a few.
17.5: And the Jews were envious and drew evil men to themselves from the streets of the city and formed a great mob; they were terrorizing the city and they came and raised them against the house of Jason and were seeking to bring them out from there to hand them over to the mob.
17.6: And when they did not find them there, they dragged Jason and the brethren who were there and they brought them to The Governor of the city as they were crying out, “These have terrorized the whole area and behold, they have come here again!”
17.7: “And this is their host, Jason, and these all oppose the commands of Caesar, while they are saying that there is another King, Yeshua.”
17.8: But the Governor of the city and all the people were alarmed when they heard these things.
17.9: And they took bail from Jason and also from the brethren and then they released them.
17.10: But the brethren immediately that night sent Paulus and Shila to the city Berea. And when they came there, they were entering a synagogue of the Jews.
17.11: For those Jews who were there were nobler than those Jews who were in Thessaloniqa, and they were hearing gladly from the word every day while they were distinguishing from Scripture whether these things were so.
17.12: And many of them believed, and so also of the Greeks, many men and notable women.
17.13: And when those Jews who were from Thessaloniqa knew that the word of God was preached by Paulus in the city Berea, they came there and did not cease to stir up and to alarm the populace.
17.14: And the brethren sent Paulus to go down to the sea and Shila and Timotheos remained in the city.
17.15: And those who accompanied Paulus came with him to Athens the city and when they left his presence, they took a letter from him to Shila and Timotheos that they should go quickly to him.
17.16: But while Paulus remained in Athens, he was provoked in his spirit when he saw that the whole city was full of idols.
17.17: And he was speaking in a synagogue with the Jews and with those who were worshipers of God and in the market place with those who gathered everyday.
17.18: Also philosophers from the school of Epicurus and others who are called Stoics were debating with him and some of them were saying, “What does this collector of words want?” And others were saying, “He is proclaiming foreign gods”, because he was proclaiming Yeshua and his resurrection to them.
17.19: And they took him and brought him to the place of judgment, which is called Arios-Pagos, while they were saying to him, “Can we know what this new teaching is that you proclaim?”
17.20: “For you have sown strange words in our hearing and we wish to know what these things are.”
17.21: But all the Athenians and those foreigners who come there are concerned about nothing except to tell and to hear something new.
17.22: And when Paulus arose in Arios-Pagos, he said, “Men, Athenians, I see that in all things you excel in the worship of daemons.”
17.23: “For as I was going around and beholding your temples, I found one altar on which it was written: “The Unknown God”; him therefore whom you do not know and yet worship, I proclaim to you.”
17.24: “For The God who made the world, and everything whatsoever is in it, and is the Lord of the Heavens and of The Earth, does not dwell in temples made with hands.”
17.25: “And he is not served by the hands of men, and he has no need of anything, because he gives every person life and a soul.”
17.26: “And from one blood he made the whole world of humanity to be dwelling on the whole surface of The Earth and he marked out the times in his decrees and set the coasts of the dwelling places of humanity,”
17.27: “So that they would be seeking and inquiring after God; and they may find him by his creation, because also he is not far from everyone of us.”
17.28: “For it is by him that we have life and we move and exist; so also some of the wise men among you have said: “Our lineage is from him.”
17.29: “Men, therefore, because our lineage is from God, we ought not to think that gold or silver or stone carved by the skill and knowledge of a man is like The Godhead.”
17.30: “For God has banished the times of deception, and at this time he commands all the children of men: 'Everyone in every place shall return to God.'”
17.31: “Because he has appointed the day in which he is going to judge the whole Earth in righteousness by the man whom he has designated and he has turned everyone to his faith in that he has raised him from among the dead.”
17.32: And when they heard of the resurrection from among the dead, some of them were mocking and some were saying, “We shall hear you another time about this.”
17.33: And so Paulus went out from among them.
17.34: And some of them joined him and believed, but one of them was Dionysius of the Judges of Areos-Pagos, and one woman whose name was Damaris, and others with them.

18.1: And when Paulus went out from Athens, he came to Corinthus.
18.2: And there he found one man, who was a Jew, whose name was Aqilaus, who was from Pontus, who at that time had come from the country of Italia, he and Priscilla his wife, because Claudius Caesar had ordered all the Jews to leave Rome, and he came to them.
18.3: And because he was a member of their craft, he stayed with them, and he was working with them in their craft, for they were Tentmakers.
18.4: And he was speaking in the synagogue on every Sabbath and was persuading the Jews and the Pagans.
18.5: And when Shila and Timotheos had come from Macedonia, Paulus was constrained in the word because the Jews were opposing him and blaspheming as he was testifying to them that Yeshua is The Messiah.
18.6: And he shook his clothes and he said to them, “From now on I am clean; I myself go to the Gentiles.”
18.7: And he left there and entered the house of a man whose name was Titus, one who was a worshiper of God, and his house was joined to the synagogue.
18.8: And Krispus, The Leader of the synagogue was trusting in Our Lord, he and all the children of his household, and many Corinthians were listening and were trusting in God and being baptized.
18.9: And THE LORD JEHOVAH said in a vision to Paulus, “Do not be afraid, but speak and do not be silent,
18.10: Because I am with you, and no man can harm you, and I have many people in the city.”
18.11: And he remained one year and six months in Corinthus and was teaching them the word of God.
18.12: And when Galion The Proconsul of Akaia was there, the Jews gathered together as one against Paulus, and they brought him before the judgment seat,
18.13: While they were saying, “This one persuades people to be worshiping God outside of The Law.”
18.14: And when Paulus had requested to open his mouth and speak, Galion said to the Jews, “If you are accusing about a matter of wickedness or fraud or what is hateful, Oh Jews, I would receive you on the merits.
18.15: But if the charges are about a discourse or about names or about your law, you know among yourselves that I do not want to be judge of these matters.”
18.16: And he expelled them from his judgment seat.
18.17: And all the pagans were seizing Sosthenes, the Elder of the synagogue, and they were beating him before the judgment seat and Galion was disregarding these things.
18.18: And when Paulus had been there for many days, he bade farewell to the brethren and he journeyed by sea to go to Syria, and Priscilla and Aqilaus came with him when he shaved his head in Qenkreos, because he had vowed a vow for himself.
18.19: And he arrived at Ephesaus and Paulus entered the synagogue and he was speaking with the Jews.
18.20: And they were asking him to tarry with them, and he did not consent,
18.21: As he said, “I must always observe the coming feast in Jerusalem, and if God wills, I shall return again to you.” And he left Aqilaus and Priscilla in Ephesaus.
18.22: He traveled by sea and came to Qesaria, and he came up and invoked the peace of the members of the church and he went on to Antiakia.
18.23: And when he was there for notable days, he went out and traveled in the countries, one after the other, of Galatia and Phrygia, establishing all of the disciples.
18.24: One man, a Jew whose name was Apollo, a native of Alexandria and instructed in the word, was familiar with the Scriptures and he came to Ephesaus.
18.25: This man had been taught the way of THE LORD JEHOVAH and he was fervent in spirit, speaking and teaching thoroughly about Yeshua, yet not knowing anything except the baptism of Yohannan.
18.26: And he began publicly speaking in the synagogue, and when Aqilaus and Priscilla heard him, they brought him unto them, and thoroughly showed him the way of THE LORD JEHOVAH.
18.27: As he wanted to go to Akaia, they exhorted the brethren and wrote to the disciples to receive him and when he went, he helped all the believers greatly by grace.
18.28: He was powerfully instructing contrary to the Jews before the crowds while showing from the Scripture concerning Yeshua, that he is The Messiah.

19.1: And when Apollo was in Corinthus, Paulus went about in the upper countries and came to Ephesaus, and was questioning those disciples whom he had found there:
19.2: “Have you received The Spirit of Holiness since you have believed?” They answered, and were saying to him, “We have not even heard if there is a Spirit of Holiness.”
19.3: And he said to them, “Unto what were you baptized?” They were saying, “Unto the baptism of Yohannan”.
19.4: Paulus said to them, “Yohannan baptized the people in the baptism of repentance while he was telling them to believe in that One who would come after him, who is Yeshua The Messiah.”
19.5: And when they heard these things, they were baptized in the name of our Lord Yeshua The Messiah.
19.6: And Paulus laid a hand on them and The Spirit of Holiness came upon them, and they were speaking in various languages and were prophesying.
19.7: But they were all of twelve men.
19.8: And Paulus was entering the synagogue and he was speaking publicly three months and persuading concerning The Kingdom of God.
19.9: Some of them were hardened and were disputing and reviling the way of God before the assembly of the Gentiles; then Paulus left and he separated the disciples from them and was speaking every day with them in the school of a man whose name was Turanos.
19.10: And this continued for two years until all who dwelt in Asia heard the word of THE LORD JEHOVAH, Jews and Aramaeans.
19.11: And God was doing great miracles by the hand of Paulus.
19.12: So also napkins or rags which were placed upon his robe or his body were brought and placed on the sick, and the diseases were departing from them; even demons were going out.
19.13: But there were also some Jewish men who were going around and were exorcists of demons, exorcising in the name of our Lord Yeshua over those who had a foul spirit in them, while saying, “We exorcise you in the name of Yeshua whom Paulus preaches.”
19.14: But there were seven sons of a man who was a Jew, a Chief Priest, whose name was Sqewa, who were doing this,
19.15: And that evil spirit answered and said to them, “I know Yeshua, and I know Paulus, but who are you?”
19.16: And that man who had the evil spirit in him jumped upon them and overpowered them and threw them down, and when they were stripped and wounded, they fled from the house.
19.17: And this became known to all the Jews and Aramaeans dwelling in Ephesaus and great fear fell upon all of them, and the name of our Lord Yeshua The Messiah was exalted.
19.18: Many of those who believed were coming and relating their wrongdoing, and they were confessing the things that they were doing.
19.19: Many sorcerers also gathered their books and brought and burned them before everyone and they calculated their price, and it came up to fifty thousand silver pieces.
19.20: And thus with great power the faith of God was increasing in strength and growing.
19.21: But when these things were done, Paulus set in his mind to journey in all Macedonia and in Akaia and to go to Jerusalem, and he said, “When I have gone there, I must also see Rome.”
19.22: And he sent two men of those who were ministering to him to Macedonia - Timotheos and Erastus, but he stayed for a time in Asia.
19.23: But there was a great commotion at that time about the way of God.
19.24: And a certain Silversmith was there whose name was Demetrius, who was making silver shrines for Artemis, and he was enriching the members of his craft with great profits.
19.25: This man gathered all members of his craft together and those who worked with them, and he said to them, “Men, you know that all of our profit is from this work.”
19.26: “You also hear and see that this Paulus has persuaded and turned away, not only the citizens of Ephesaus, but also the multitudes of all Asia, when he said, “Those are not gods which are made by the hands of men.”
19.27: “Neither is this matter only exposed and finished, but so also is The Temple of Artemis, the great goddess, reckoned as nothing. And even this goddess, whom all Asia and all nations worship, is despised.”
19.28: And when they heard these things, they were filled with rage and were crying out and saying, “Great is Artemis of the Ephesians!”
19.29: And the entire city was stirred up and ran as one and went to the theater, and they took by force and brought with them Gaius and Aristarkus, Macedonian men and companions of Paulus.
19.30: And Paulus had wanted to enter the theater and the disciples restrained him.
19.31: Even the Rulers of Asia, because they loved him, sent and begged him not to offer himself to enter the theater.
19.32: But the crowds that were in the theater were very chaotic and they were shouting to each other, but many of them did not know why they had assembled.
19.33: But the people of the Judeans who were there put forth a man of them, a Jew whose name was Alexandrus, and as he arose, he beckoned with his hand and wanted to put forth a defense to the people.
19.34: And when they knew that he was a Jew, they all shouted with one voice for about two hours: “Great is Artemis of the Ephesians!”
19.35: And the Governor of the city pacified them and he said, “Men, Ephesians! Who of the children of men does not know of the city of the Ephesians and of the temple worship of the great Artemis and the image that descended from Heaven?”
19.36: “Because therefore no one can speak against this, you must be quiet and not do anything hasty.”
19.37: “For you have brought these men although they have not robbed temples neither have reviled our goddess.”
19.38: “But if this Demetrius and the fellows of his trade have a judgment with any, behold, the Proconsuls of the city are skilled; let them approach and dispute one with another.”
19.39: “And if you seek anything other than what is granted by the law, the assembly will be dismissed.”
19.40: “For even now we are in danger of being accused as seditious, so that we will not be able to offer a defense for the crowd today, because we have assembled needlessly and we are in an uproar without a cause.”
19.41: And when he has said these things, he dismissed the multitude.

20.1: And after the uproar had ceased, Paulus called the disciples and comforted them and kissed them, and he departed and went to Macedonia.
20.2: And when he traveled those regions and comforted them with many words, he came to the country of Greece.
20.3: And he was there three months, but the Jews made a plot against him when he was going to leave for Syria and planned to return to Macedonia.
20.4: And Supatros went out with him to Asia, who was from the city Berea, and Aristarkus and Sequndus, who were from Thessalon?qa, and Gaius, who was from the city Derby, and Timotheos, who was from Lystra, and Tykiqos and Trophimos from Asia.
20.5: These went before us and waited for us in Troas.
20.6: But we departed from Philippus, the city of Macedonia, after the days of unleavened bread, and we went by sea and came to Troas in five days and remained there seven days.
20.7: In the first day of the week, when we assembled to break the Eucharist, Paulus was speaking with them, because the next day he was going to go out by himself and he prolonged speaking until midnight.
20.8: And there were many fire lamps there in an upper room in which we were gathered.
20.9: And there was a young man whose name was Eutikus, sitting there in a window, and he listened and he sank into a deep sleep, and as Paulus had prolonged the message, in his sleep he fell from the third story, and he was taken up as dead.
20.10: And Paulus came down and fell upon him and embraced him and he said, “Do not be troubled, because his life is in him.”
20.11: But when he got up, he broke bread and ate, and he spoke until sunrise, and then he went out to depart by land.
20.12: And they brought the youth alive, and they rejoiced over him greatly.
20.13: But we went into the ship and we sailed to the port of Thesos, because from there we prepared to receive Paulus, for thus he had commanded us when he had gone on by land.
20.14: And when we had received him from Thesos, we took him into the ship and came to Mitolina.
20.15: And from there the next day we sailed next to Kios the island, and again the next day we came to Samos and we stayed in Trogulion and the next day we came to Miletus,
20.16: For Paulus was determined to pass by it to Ephesaus, lest he be delayed there because he was hurrying, that if he were able, he would keep the day of Pentecost in Jerusalem.
20.17: And from Miletus he sent and brought the Elders of the Church of Ephesaus.
20.18: And when they came to him, he said to them, “You are aware that from the first day that I entered Asia, how I have been with you all the time,”
20.19: “As I served God in much humility and in tears, and in those trials that have come upon me by the treachery of the Jews.”
20.20: “And I have neglected nothing that was useful for your souls, to preach and to teach in the marketplace and in houses,”
20.21: “While I was testifying to the Jews and to the Aramaeans about returning home to the Presence of God and the faith in our Lord Yeshua The Messiah.”
20.22: “And now I am shackled by The Spirit, and I go on to Jerusalem, and I do not know what will meet me there.”
20.23: “However, The Spirit of Holiness testifies in every city to me and he says, 'Chains and afflictions are prepared for you.'”
20.24: “But my life is esteemed nothing to me, so that I may finish my race course and the ministry which I have received from our Lord Yeshua, to testify of The Gospel of the grace of God.”
20.25: “And now I know that none of you will see my face again, all of you among whom I have gone and preached to you The Kingdom.”
20.26: “And because of this, I testify to you today that I am pure from the blood of all of you.”
20.27: “For I have not declined to show you the whole will of God.”
20.28: “Pay attention to yourselves therefore, and to the whole flock in which The Spirit of Holiness has appointed you overseers, to shepherd the Church of God which he has purchased with his blood.”
20.29: “For I know that after I go, powerful wolves will enter with you that will not spare the flock.”
20.30: “And even some of you and your own men will arise, speaking perversions so as to turn the disciples to go after them.”
20.31: “Therefore be vigilant, and remember that for three years I have not been silent by night and by day, while with tears I was warning each one of you.”
20.32: “And now I commit you to God and to the word of his grace, which is able to build you up, and to give you an inheritance with all of The Holy Ones.”
20.33: “I have not coveted silver, gold, or apparel.”
20.34: “And you know that these my hands have ministered to my needs and to those who were with me.”
20.35: “And I have shown you everything, that thus it is necessary to labor and to take care of those who are weak and to remember the words of our Lord Yeshua, who said, 'He who gives is more blessed than he who receives.'”
20.36: And when he had said these things, he knelt on his knees and he prayed, and all the men with him.
20.37: And there was great weeping among all of them, and they were embracing him and kissing him.
20.38: But they were all the more pained about that statement which he said, that they were not going to see his face again; and they accompanied him to the ship.

21.1: And we departed from them and we traveled straight to the Isle Qo, and the next day we came to Rhodus, and from there to Patara.
21.2: And we found a ship there going to Phoenicia and we boarded it and sailed.
21.3: We came as far as to The Island Cyprus, and we passed it to the left and we came to Syria, and from there we arrived at Tyre, for the ship was to unload its cargo there.
21.4: And when we found disciples there, we stayed with them seven days, and they were saying to Paulus everyday by The Spirit not to go to Jerusalem.
21.5: And after these days we went out to go by road, and they were all following us with their wives and their children to the outside of the city. And they knelt on their knees by the seaside, and they prayed.
21.6: And we kissed one another, and we boarded the ship, and they returned to their homes.
21.7: And we journeyed from Tyre and we came to the city Akko, and we gave greeting to the brethren there and we lodged with them one day.
21.8: And the next day we departed and came to Caesarea, and we entered and lodged in the house of Philippus The Evangelist, he who was of the seven.
21.9: And he had four virgin daughters who did prophesy.
21.10: And when we were there many days, a certain Prophet had come down from Judea, whose name was Agabus.
21.11: And he came to us and took off the leather belt on the waist of Paulus and he tied his own feet and his hands and he said, “Thus says The Spirit of Holiness: 'The Jews in Jerusalem will bind and deliver the man who owns this leather belt into the hands of the Gentiles.' “
21.12: And when we heard these words we begged of him, we and the people of that place, that he would not go to Jerusalem.
21.13: Then Paulus answered and said, “What are you doing that you are weeping and breaking my heart? For I am ready not only to be bound, but also to die in Jerusalem for the name of our Lord Yeshua.”
21.14: And when he was not persuaded by us, we ceased, and we said, “The will of our Lord be done.”
21.15: After these days, we prepared and we ourselves went up to Jerusalem.
21.16: And men who were disciples from Caesarea came with us as they took with them a brother to receive us into his house, one of the first disciples, whose name was Mnason, and he was from Cyprus.
21.17: And when we came to Jerusalem, the brethren received us gladly.
21.18: And the next day we entered with Paulus unto Yaqob, while all the Elders were there with him.
21.19: And we gave them greeting and Paulus was reporting in order to them everything that God had done among the Gentiles by his ministry,
21.20: And when they had heard, they praised God and said to him: “You see our brother, how many tens of thousands are in Judea who have believed, and they are all zealous of The Law.”
21.21: “But it has been told them that you teach all the Jews who are among the Gentiles to separate from Moses while you say that they should not circumcise their sons and that they should not walk by the custom of The Law.”
21.22: “Therefore, because this has been heard by them, they have come here.”
21.23: “Do what we tell you; we have four men who have vowed to purify themselves.”
21.24: “Take them and go be purified with them and pay the cost with them to shave their heads, and it will be known to everyone that whatever was spoken about you is false, and that you fulfill and keep The Law.”
21.25: “But concerning those who believe among the Gentiles, we have written that they would keep themselves from what is sacrificed, from fornication, from strangled things and from blood.”
21.26: Then Paulus took those men and the next day was purified with them and they entered and went to The Temple, showing them the fulfillment of the days of purification until an offering was offered by each one of them.
21.27: And when the seventh day arrived, the Jews of Asia saw him in The Temple, and they incited all the people against him and they laid hands upon him,
21.28: As they appealed and they were saying, “Men, sons of Israel, help! This is the man who opposes our people, teaching in every place against the law and against this place, and he also has brought an Aramaean into The Temple and has defiled this holy place.”
21.29: For they had seen Trophimos the Ephesian with him before in the city and they were assuming that he had entered The Temple with Paulus.
21.30: And the entire city was stirred up and all the people assembled and they seized Paulus and they dragged him outside of The Temple and immediately the gates were shut.
21.31: And while the crowd was seeking to kill him, it was heard by the Chiliarch of the regiment that the whole city had been stirred up.
21.32: And immediately he took a Centurion and many Soldiers and ran unto them, and when they had seen the Chiliarch and the Soldiers, they ceased beating Paulus.
21.33: And the Chiliarch called him and took him and commanded to bind him with two chains, and he was asking about him, who he was and what he had done.
21.34: And men from the mob were shouting this and that about him, and because of their shouting, he was unable to know what the truth was, and commanded to bring him to the encampment.
21.35: And when Paulus came to the stairs, the Soldiers carried him because of the violence of the people;
21.36: For many people were coming after him and shouting, and they were saying, “Hang him!”
21.37: And when he approached to enter the encampment, Paulus said to the Chiliarch, “If you allow me, I shall speak with you”, but he said to him, “Do you know Greek?”
21.38: “Are you not that Egyptian who before these days made a disturbance and led four thousand criminal men to the wilderness?”
21.39: Paulus said to him, “I am a man, a Jew from Tarsus, the notable city of Qiliqia in which I was born. I beg of you, let me speak to the people.”
21.40: And when he allowed him, Paulus stood on the stairs and was motioning to them with his hand, and when they were quiet he spoke with them in Judean Aramaic, and said to them:

22.1: “Brothers and fathers, hear my defense unto you.”
22.2: And when they heard that he was speaking Judean Aramaic with them, they were all the more quiet, and he said to them:
22.3: “I was raised in this city at the feet of Gamaliel, and I was instructed perfectly in the tradition of our fathers, and I am zealous for God, as also are all of you.”
22.4: “And I persecuted this way unto death, as I would bind and deliver men and women into prison.”
22.5: “And according to what The High Priest witnessed of me, and all the Elders from whom I received warrants to go to the brethren who are in Darmsuq, so I also brought those who were there to Jerusalem as prisoners and they would receive capital punishment.”
22.6: “And as I was going and began to approach Darmsuq at midday, under a quiet sky, a great light burst upon me.”
22.7: And I fell to the ground, and I heard a voice saying to me, “Shaul, Shaul, why do you persecute me?”
22.8: “But I answered, and I said, “Who are you, my lord?” And he said to me, “I am Yeshua the Nazarene, whom you are persecuting.”
22.9: “And the men who were with me saw the light, but they did not hear the voice which was speaking to me.”
22.10: And I said, “What shall I do, my lord?” And our Lord said to me, “Rise, go to Darmsuq, and there will be spoken with you concerning everything that you will be commanded to do.”
22.11: “And when I could see nothing because of the glory of that light, they who were with me held me by the hands, and I went to Darmsuq.”
22.12: “And one man, Khanan-Yah, righteous in The Law, as all the Jews were testifying there about him”,
22.13: “Came to me and said to me, 'Shaul, my brother, open your eyes', and at that moment my eyes were opened and I saw.”
22.14: And he said to me, “The God of our fathers has appointed you to know his will and to see The Righteous One and hear the voice of his mouth.”
22.15: “And you shall be a witness for him to all people about everything whatsoever you have seen and heard.”
22.16: “And now why do you wait? Arise, be baptized, and be cleansed from your sins while you call upon his name.”
22.17: “And I returned and came here to Jerusalem, and I prayed in The Temple.”
22.18: “And I saw in a vision as he said to me, 'Hurry and depart from Jerusalem, because they do not receive your testimony about me.' “
22.19: And I said, “My Lord, they also know that I have delivered to prison and beaten in all our synagogues those who have believed in you.”
22.20: “And when the blood of your witness Estephanos was shed, I also was standing with them and consenting to the will of those who killed him, and I was holding the garments of those who were stoning him.”
22.21: And he said to me, “Go, for I am sending you far away to preach to the Gentiles.”
22.22: And when they had heard Paulus up until this statement, they raised their voice and shouted, “Hang such a person above The Earth, for it is not right for him to live!”
22.23: And as they were yelling and throwing off their garments and were casting up dust toward the sky,
22.24: The Chiliarch commanded to take him to the encampment and ordered that he be questioned by scourging, so as to know for what cause they were crying out against him.
22.25: And when they stretched him with leather straps, Paulus said to the Centurion who was standing over him, “Are you allowed to scourge a Roman who has not been condemned?”
22.26: And when the Centurion heard this, he called to the Chiliarch and he said to him, “What have you done? This man is a Roman!”
22.27: And the Chiliarch came to him and he said to him, “Tell me”, are you a Roman?” And he said to him, “Yes.”
22.28: And the Chiliarch answered and said, “I bought Roman citizenship with much money.” Paulus said to him, “But I was born with it.”
22.29: And at once those who were seeking to scourge him withdrew from him, and the Chiliarch was afraid when he learned that he was a Roman, for he had bound him.
22.30: And the next day, he had wanted to really know what the accusation was that the Jews had brought against him, and he released him and he had commanded The Chief Priests and all The Council of their Rulers to come, and he led Paulus and brought him down and stood him in the midst of them.

23.1: And Paulus gazed at The Assembly and he said, “Men, brothers: in all good conscience I have lived before God, until today.”
23.2: And Khanan-Yah The Priest commanded those who stood on the side to hit Paulus on his mouth.
23.3: And Paulus said to him, “God is going to strike you, you whitened wall, and are you sitting to judge me according to the law, when you violate the law and command to strike me?
23.4: And those who stood there said to him, “You accuse God's Priest?”
23.5: Paulus said to them, “I was not aware, brothers, that he is The Priest, for it is written: 'Do not curse The Ruler of your people.' “
23.6: And when Paulus knew that some of the people were Sadducees and some Pharisees, he was shouting in The Council, “Men, brothers; I am a Pharisee, son of a Pharisee, and for the hope of the resurrection of the dead I am being judged.”
23.7: When he had said this, The Pharisees and The Sadducees fell one upon the other, and the group was divided.
23.8: For The Sadducees were saying there is no resurrection, neither Angel, nor spirit, but The Pharisees confess all of these.
23.9: And there was a great noise. Some of the Scribes stood on the side of The Pharisees and were contending with them and they were saying, “We find no evil in this man, but if a spirit or an Angel has spoken with him, what is there in that?”
23.10: And when there was a great uproar among them, the Chiliarch was afraid lest they would tear Paulus apart, and he sent Romans to go snatch him from their midst and bring him to the encampment.
23.11: When it was night, our Lord appeared to Paulus and said to him, “Be strong, because as you testified of me in Jerusalem, thus you are going also to testify in Rome.”
23.12: And when it was dawn, some of the Jews gathered together and put a curse upon themselves that they would neither eat nor drink until they would kill Paulus.
23.13: But those who established this covenant with an oath were more than forty men.
23.14: And they came to The Priests and to the Elders and were saying, “We have put a curse upon ourselves that we shall not taste anything until we shall kill Paulus.”
23.15: “And now you and the Rulers of The Council, ask the Chiliarch to bring him to you as if you seek to examine his conduct properly, and we are ready to kill him before he arrives to you.”
23.16: And the son of Paulus' sister heard this plot, and he entered the encampment and informed Paulus.
23.17: And Paulus sent and called one of the Centurions and said to him, “Escort this young man to the Chiliarch, for he has something to tell him.”
23.18: And the Centurion led the youth and brought him to the Chiliarch and said, “Paulus the prisoner called me and begged me to bring this young man to you, who has something to say to you.”
23.19: And the Chiliarch took the youth by the hand and drew him to one side and was asking him, “What do you have to say to me?”
23.20: And the youth said to him, “The Judeans have planned to ask you to send Paulus down tomorrow to their Council as if they want to learn something more from him;”
23.21: “Therefore you should not believe them, for behold, there are more than forty men of them who are watching for him in ambush and they have put a curse upon themselves that they will not eat or drink until they kill him. And behold, they are ready and waiting for your promise.”
23.22: And the Chiliarch dismissed the youth as he commanded him: “Let no one know that you have shown me these things.”
23.23: And he called two Centurions and said to them, “Go prepare two hundred Romans to go to Caesarea and seventy horsemen and two hundred right-handed spearmen to go out from the third hour in the night.”
23.24: “But prepare also beasts of burden so that they may mount Paulus and let him escape to Felix, the Governor.”
23.25: And he wrote a letter and he gave it to those who were with him thus:
23.26: “Qlaudius Lucius to Felix the Governor, the Excellent: Greeting;
23.27: “The Judeans seized this man so as to kill him, and I helped with the Romans and saved him when I learned that he is a Roman.”
23.28: “And when I sought to know the cause for which they were accusing him, I brought him down to their Council.”
23.29: “And I found that they were accusing him about charges of their law, and there was no cause worthy for imprisonment or death against him.”
23.30: “And when it was shown me that the Judeans made a plot by ambush against him, I sent him at once to you and ordered his accusers to go and speak with him before you. Farewell.”
23.31: Then the Romans brought Paulus in the night, as they were ordered, and brought him to the city AntiPatris.
23.32: And the next day the horsemen dismissed the Foot Soldiers, their comrades, to return to camp.
23.33: And they brought him to Caesarea and gave the letter to the Governor and presented Paulus before him.
23.34: And when he read the letter, he was asking him from which province he was, and when he learned that he was from Qiliqia,
23.35: He said to him, “I shall hear you whenever your accusers have come.” And he commanded to keep him in the Praetorium of Herodus.

24.1: After five days Khanan-Yah The High Priest came down with the Elders and Tertullos the orator and they informed the Governor concerning Paulus.
24.2: And when he was called, Tertullos began to accuse him and said, “With the abundance of tranquility we dwell because of you, and this people have much excellent stability in receiving your care.”
24.3: “And we all in every place receive your grace, excellent Felix.”
24.4: “But that we may not weary you with many things, I beg of you to hear our lowliness briefly.”
24.5: “We have found this man, who is a corruptor and agitator of tumult to all the Jews who are in every land, for he is the leader of the doctrine of The Nazarene.”
24.6: “And he wanted to defile our Temple, and when we seized him, we sought to judge him according to what is in our law.”
24.7: “But Lucius the Chiliarch came and with great violence snatched him from our hands and sent him to you.”
24.8: “And he commanded his accusers to come to you, and when you question him you can learn from him concerning all these things of which we accuse him.”
24.9: But those Jews were crying out concerning him, as they were saying, “These things are so!”
24.10: And the Governor beckoned to Paulus to speak, and Paulus answered and said: “I know that you are the Judge of this nation for many years; because of this, I gladly render a defense for myself.”
24.11: “As you may know, it is not more than twelve days since I came up to Jerusalem to worship,
24.12: Neither did they find me speaking with any man in The Temple nor a crowd that I had gathered, either in their synagogues, nor in the city;”
24.13: “Neither can they demonstrate concerning anything of which they accuse me now.”
24.14: “And yet I confess this, that in the same doctrine of which they speak, I serve The God of my fathers, as I believe all things which are written in The Written Law and in The Prophets.”
24.15: “And while I have hope in God, which they also do, they preach these things: There is going to be a resurrection from the dead, of the righteous and of the evil.”
24.16: “Because of this, I also labor that my conscience may always be pure before God and before all people.”
24.17: “But for many years I have come before my people to give charity and to offer gifts.”
24.18: “And these men found me in The Temple when I had been purified, not with the crowds, neither with a tumult.”
24.19: “But the Jews who came up from Asia, who stirred up the people, ought to be standing with me before you to bring whatever charge they have,”
24.20: “Or let them say what offense they have found in me when I stood before their Council,”
24.21: “Except for this statement, which I cried when I stood in their midst: 'For the resurrection of the dead I am judged today before you.'”
24.22: But because Felix knew this way fully, he deferred them when he said, “When the Chiliarch comes, I shall hear between you.”
24.23: And the Centurion ordered to guard Paulus at ease and that no one of his acquaintances would be forbidden to minister to him.
24.24: And after a few days, Felix, and Dursilla his Jewish wife, sent and called Paulus and heard from him concerning the faith of The Messiah,
24.25: And as he was speaking with them of righteousness, of holiness and of the judgment that was to come, Felix was afraid and he said, “Go now, and when I have a space I will send for you.”
24.26: For he had hoped that a bribe would have been given to him by Paulus, because of this, also, he was continually sending to bring him and speaking with him.
24.27: And when two years were completed, the next Governor had come in his place, who is called Porqius-Festus, but Felix, in order to do a favor for the Jews, left Paulus as a prisoner.

25.1: And when Festus came to Caesarea, after three days he came up to Jerusalem.
25.2: And The Chief Priests and the Rulers of the Jews informed him concerning Paulus and they were inquiring of him,
25.3: When they asked him this favor: to send to bring him to Jerusalem, as they were making an ambush by the road to kill him.
25.4: And Festus returned the answer: “Paulus is kept in Caesarea, and I am in a hurry to travel.”
25.5: “Those therefore among you who are able and have the accusations with them, let them go down with us; let them accuse the man.”
25.6: And when he was there eight or 10 days, he went down to Caesarea and the next day he sat on the judgment seat and commanded to bring Paulus.
25.7: And when he came, the Jews that descended from Jerusalem surrounded him and many Leaders had brought hard things against him, things that they were unable to demonstrate.
25.8: And when Paulus rendered a defense that he had not violated anything, not the law of the Jews, neither of The Temple, neither Caesar,
25.9: Festus, because he wanted to confer a favor on the Jews, said to Paulus, “Will you go up to Jerusalem and be judged there of these things before me?”
25.10: Paulus answered and said, “I stand at the judgment seat of Caesar. It is right for me to be judged here. I have not sinned against the Jews, as you also know.”
25.11: “If any offense was done by me or anything worthy of death, I do not excuse myself from death, but if there is nothing to these things of which they accuse me, no man may give me to them as a gift. I call for an appeal to Caesar.”
25.12: And Festus spoke with his Counselors and he said, “You have called for an appeal to Caesar; to Caesar you will go.”
25.13: And when some days passed, Agrippa The King and Bernice came down to Caesarea to inquire the welfare of Festus.
25.14: And when they were with him some days, Festus related to The King the case of Paulus, when he said, “One man is a prisoner left from the hands of Felix.”
25.15: “When I was in Jerusalem, The Chief Priests and Elders of the Jews informed me of him and requested that I make a judgment of him for them.”
25.16: “And I said to them, 'It is not the custom of the Romans to give a man for slaughter as a favor until his adversary at law shall come and blame him to his face and he shall be given an opportunity to render a defense concerning that of which he is accused.”
25.17: “And when I had come here, without delay the next day I sat on the judgment seat and I ordered them to bring the man to me.”
25.18: “And his accusers stood with him and they could not demonstrate any evil indictment against him like that which I had supposed.”
25.19: “But they had inquiries of one thing or another about their religion and about Yeshua, a man who had died, who Paulus was saying is alive.”
25.20: Because I was not certain about such inquiries, I said to Paulus, “Do you wish to go to Jerusalem, and be judged on these things there?”
25.21: “But he requested to be kept for the judgment of Caesar, and I ordered that he be kept until I send him to Caesar.”
25.22: And Agrippa said, “I would like to hear this man.” And Festus said, “You will hear him tomorrow.”
25.23: And the next day Agrippa came and Bernice, and they entered the court with great pomp with the Chiliarch and the Leaders of the city, and Festus commanded, and Paulus came.
25.24: And Festus said, “King Agrippa, and all of you men who are with us concerning this man, whom you see: all the people of the Jews complained to me in Jerusalem and here, while shouting that this man ought not to live any longer,
25.25: And I have found nothing worthy of death that has been done by him, and because he has asked to be kept for the judgment of Caesar, I have commanded that he be sent.”
25.26: “And I do not know what to write to Caesar about him, therefore I decided to bring him before you, and especially before you, King Agrippa, that when his case is examined, I may find what to write.”
25.27: For it is not right that when we send a man as a prisoner that we do not write his offense.

26.1: And Agrippa said to Paulus, “You are permitted to speak for yourself.” And Paulus stretched out his hand and offered a defense and said:
26.2: “Concerning everything of which I am accused by the Jews, King Agrippa, I consider myself blessed, because before you today I bring a defense.”
26.3: “I especially know that you are capable in all inquiries of The Law of the Jews; I therefore ask of you that you will hear me patiently.”
26.4: “For those Jews are also aware, if they wish to testify, of my way of life from my youth from the beginning, in my nation and in Jerusalem,”
26.5: “Because for a long time they have been persuaded concerning me, for they know that I was living in the official doctrine of the Pharisees.”
26.6: “Now for the hope of The Promise which our fathers had from God, I stand and I am judged.”
26.7: “For to this hope our twelve tribes hope to arrive by diligent prayer by day and night. It is for this hope I am accused by the agency of the Jews, King Agrippa.”
26.8: “How do you judge? Ought we not believe that God raises the dead?”
26.9: “For I had previously set my mind that I would commit many things contrary to the name of Yeshua the Nazarene.”
26.10: “This I did also in Jerusalem, and I cast many Saints into prison by the authority I received from The Chief Priests, and as they were being killed by them, I took part with those who condemned them.”
26.11: “And I was torturing them in every Synagogue as I was pressing them to blaspheme the name of Yeshua, and I was filled with great rage against them. I was also going out to other cities to persecute them.”
26.12: “And while I was going for this cause to Darmsuq with the authority and approval of The Chief Priests,”
26.13: “At noonday, Oh, King, while on the road, I saw a light from the heavens greater than that of the sun, which burst upon me, and upon all who were with me.”
26.14: And we all fell upon the ground, and I heard a voice, which said to me in Judean Aramaic, “Shaul, Shaul, why do you persecute me? It is hard for you to kick the goads.”
26.15: “And I said, “Who are you my lord?” And our Lord said to me, 'I am Yeshua the Nazarene, whom you are persecuting.' “
26.16: “And he said to me, 'Rise to your feet; for this cause I have appeared to you, to appoint you a minister and a witness that you have seen me and are going to see me.”
26.17: “But I shall save you from the people of the Judeans and from the other nations to which I am sending you,”
26.18: “That you will open their eyes, so that they will turn from the darkness to the light and from the authority of Satan to God; and they shall receive release from sins and a portion with The Holy Ones by the faith which is in me.”
26.19: “Because of this, King Agrippa, I did not stand in dispute against the Heavenly vision.”
26.20: “But from the first I preached to those in Darmsuq, and to those in Jerusalem and those in all the villages of Judea, and I preached also to the Gentiles that they repent and be converted to God and to do deeds worthy of a return to God.”
26.21: “And because of these things, the Jews seized me in The Temple and wanted to kill me.”
26.22: “But God has helped me until this day and behold, I stand and testify to small and to great, while I have not said anything outside The Law of Moses and The Prophets; only those things which they have said were going to happen:”
26.23: “That The Messiah would suffer and that he would be The Origin of the Resurrection from among the dead and would preach light to the nation and to the Gentiles.”
26.24: “When Paulus had thus rendered a defense, Festus cried out with a loud voice, “You are insane, Paul! Much study has made you insane!”
26.25: Paulus said, “I am not insane, excellent Festus, but I am speaking words of truth and integrity.”
26.26: “Also King Agrippa especially knows of such things as these, therefore I speak openly before him, because I do not think one of these matters has escaped him, for they were not done in secret.”
26.27: “Do you believe The Prophets, King Agrippa? I know that you do believe.”
26.28: Agrippa said to him, “In a little bit you will persuade me to become a Christian.”
26.29: Paulus said, “I have desired from God in a little and in much, not for you only, but also for all those who are hearing me now to be like me, except for these chains.”
26.30: “And The King stood for him, and the Governor and Berniqa and those who were sitting with them.”
26.31: “And when they withdrew from there, they were speaking one with another and were saying, “This man has not done any thing worthy of death or imprisonment.”
26.32: And Agrippa said to Festus, “This man could have been released if he had not appealed to Caesar.”

27.1: And Festus commanded concerning him to be sent to Caesar to Italia and he delivered Paulus and the other prisoners with him to a certain Centurion from the regiment, Sebasta, whose name was Julius.
27.2: And when it was time for us to journey, we boarded a ship, which was from the city Adramantius and it was going to the region of Asia, and Aristarkus, a Macedonian, entered the ship with us who was from the city Thessalon?qa.
27.3: The next day we came to Sidon and the Centurion treated Paulus with compassion and allowed him to go to his friends and be refreshed.
27.4: And from there we sailed, and because the wind was against us, we went around unto Cyprus.
27.5: And we passed through The Sea of Qiliqia and of Pamphylia, and we arrived at Mura, a city of Luqia.
27.6: And the Centurion found a ship there from Alexandria that was going to Italia and he put us on it.
27.7: And because it was hardly moving for many days, laboring we came next to the island Qnidus, and because the wind would not permit us to go straight, we went around to Crete, opposite the city Salmona.
27.8: And we labored as we sailed around it and we came to a place called Lemana-Shappira and it was near a city whose name was Lasea.
27.9: We were there for a long time until the day of the fast of the Jews had passed, and it was fearful for a man to travel by sea, and Paulus was counseling them,
27.10: And he said, “Men, I have seen that our voyage will be with calamity and great loss, not only of the cargo of our ship, but also of ourselves.”
27.11: But the Centurion was listening to the Helmsman and the Shipmaster rather than to the words of Paulus.
27.12: And because it was not suitable to winter in that port, many of us wanted to journey from there, and if it was possible for us, to arrive and to winter in a certain port which was in Crete, called Phoenix; and it faces toward the south.
27.13: And when the south wind blew and they hoped to arrive according to their desire, they went around Crete.
27.14: And after a little while, the wind of a hurricane came upon us called “Typhoniqos Euroqlydon”.
27.15: And the ship was carried by force and could not stand against the wind and we surrendered to its power.
27.16: And when we passed an island called Qeuda, we were scarcely able to hold the lifeboat.
27.17: And when we took it up, we girded it and retained it to the ship, because we were afraid lest it fall in a declivity of the sea, and we took down the sail, and so we moved on.
27.18: And when the storm arose upon us, it was severe; the next day we threw goods into the sea.
27.19: And the third day, we cast away the implements of the ship with our hands.
27.20: And as the storm held it for many days and the sun had not appeared, neither the moon nor the stars, all hope for our lives was entirely cut off.
27.21: And when no one was able to endure the situation, then Paulus arose in their midst and said, “If you men had believed me, we would not have sailed from Crete, and we would have been spared this loss and this suffering.”
27.22: “And now I counsel that we shall be without harm, for the life of none of you will be lost, but only the ship.”
27.23: “For tonight, an Angel of The God whose I am and whom I serve, appeared to me”,
27.24: “And he said to me, 'Do not fear, Paul, for you will stand before Caesar and, behold, God has given to you as a gift all who travel with you.' “
27.25: “Therefore, take heart, men, for I believe God, that it is so according to what was spoken to me.”
27.26: “However, we must be cast upon a certain island.”
27.27: And after fourteen days, we wandered and we were buffeted in the Hadrian Sea; at midnight, the Sailors thought that they were approaching land.
27.28: And they cast an anchor and they found twenty fathoms and they journeyed again a little more and they found fifteen fathoms.
27.29: And we were afraid lest we would be found in places that have rocks in them; we cast four anchors from the prow of the ship, and we were praying that day would come.
27.30: But the Sailors sought to flee the ship and lowered the lifeboat from it to the sea, in the pretense that they would go in it and tie the ship to land.
27.31: And when Paul saw it, he told the Centurion and the Soldiers: “If these do not stay in the ship, you cannot survive.”
27.32: And the Soldiers cut the ropes of the lifeboat from the ship, and they left it adrift.
27.33: And while it was yet morning, Paulus persuaded all of them to take food, as he said to them, “Behold, today it is the fourteenth day of peril and you have eaten nothing.”
27.34: “Therefore, I beg you to take food for the sustenance of your lives, for not a hair of any one of your heads will perish.”
27.35: And when he said these things, he took bread and praised God before all of them, and he broke it and began to eat.
27.36: And they were all comforted and received nourishment.
27.37: And we in the ship were two hundred and seventy six souls.
27.38: And when they were filled with food, they lightened the ship, and they took wheat and threw it into the sea.
27.39: And when it was day, the Mariners did not know what land it was, but they saw beside the dry land a certain bay of the sea where they were wondering if it were possible to drive the ship.
27.40: And they cut the anchors from the ship and left them in the sea and loosed the rudder bands of the rudder, lifted the small top sail to capture the wind, and they were sailing toward dry land.
27.41: “And the ship touched a prominence between two deep channels of the sea, and it was stuck in it and stopped upon its front end, and the end of it did not move, but the stern was destroyed by the force of the waves.
27.42: The Soldiers had wanted to kill the prisoners, lest they would take to swimming and escape from them.
27.43: And the Centurion denied them this because he wanted to save Paulus, and those who were able to take to swimming he commanded first to swim and to pass on to land.
27.44: And they sent the rest of them on boards and on other wood of the ship, and thus all of them escaped to land.

28.1: And after this we learned that this island was called Melita.
28.2: And the Barbarians who were dwelling in it showed us great kindness and kindled a fire and called us all to warm ourselves, because there was much cold rain.
28.3: And Paulus took a bunch of sticks and set them on the fire and a viper came out of them from the heat of the fire, and it bit his hand.
28.4: And when the Barbarians saw it hanging on his hand, they were saying, “Perhaps this man is a murderer who, while he has escaped from the sea, justice would not let live.”
28.5: Then Paulus shook his hand and threw the viper into the fire, and nothing bad happened to him.
28.6: But the Barbarians were thinking that he would immediately swell up and drop dead on the ground. When they had waited for a long time and saw that no evil effect had occurred to him, they changed their talk and they said, “He is a god.”
28.7: But there was in that place a village belonging to a man whose name was Puplios, who was The Chief of the island, and he joyfully received us into his house for three days.
28.8: The father of Puplios had a fever and was ill with a disease of the intestines and Paulus entered his presence and prayed and laid his hand upon him and healed him.
28.9: And when this happened, the rest of those who were sick in the island were coming to him, and they were healed.
28.10: And they honored us greatly, and when we were leaving from there, they loaded us with provisions.
28.11: But we went out after three months and journeyed on an Alexandrian ship which had harbored at that island, and it had on it the sign of The Twins.
28.12: And we came to the city Syracuse and remained there for three days.
28.13: And from there, we traveled around and came to the city, Rhegion; after one day, the wind blew for us from the south and in two days we came to Putielos, a city of Italia.
28.14: And we found brethren there and they begged of us and we stayed there seven days and then we went on to Rome.
28.15: And when the brethren there heard, they came forth to meet us at the Forum, which is called Appius-Forus, and unto The Three Taverns, and when Paulus saw them, he praised God and he was encouraged.
28.16: And we entered Rome and the Centurion allowed Paulus to dwell where he wanted with the Soldier who was guarding him.
28.17: And after three days, Paulus sent to call the Rulers of the Jews. When they assembled, he said to them, “Men, brothers, while in nothing have I opposed the people or the law of my fathers, I was handed over in chains from Jerusalem into the hands of the Romans.”
28.18: “And when they had examined me, they wished to release me, because the Rulers found nothing in me deserving death.”
28.19: “And as the Jews were opposing me, I was compelled to call for an appeal to Caesar, not as if I had any accusation against the children of my people.”
28.20: “Therefore, I begged of you to come and to see you and relate to you these things, because for the hope of Israel I am bound in this chain.”
28.21: And they said to him, “We have not received a letter about you from Judea, neither has anyone of the brethren who came from Jerusalem told us anything evil about you.”
28.22: “But we desire to hear from you what you think, because we know this teaching is not accepted by anyone.”
28.23: And they appointed a day for him, and they gathered and many came to him where he was dwelling. And he revealed to them concerning The Kingdom of God as he testified and persuaded them concerning Yeshua from The Law of Moses and from The Prophets, from morning until evening.
28.24: And some of them were persuaded by his words and others were not persuaded.
28.25: And they were dismissed from his presence, not agreeing with one another, and Paulus spoke this word to them: “Well spoke The Spirit of Holiness, by the mouth of Isaiah The Prophet against your fathers,
28.26: saying, "Go to this people and say to them, “Hearing, you will hear and you will not understand, and you will see and you will not observe.
28.27: The heart of this people has become hard, their hearing they have dulled and their eyes they have shut, lest they shall see with their eyes and hear with their ears and understand in their hearts and turn to me and I would forgive them.'”
28.28: “Know this, therefore: This salvation of God is sent to the Gentiles, for they are listening to it.”
28.29: And when he had said these things, the Jews went forth and many were debating among themselves.
28.30: And Paulus himself lived in his own hired house and was in it for two years; and he was receiving there all of those who were coming to him.
28.31: And he was preaching about The Kingdom of God, and teaching publicly about our Lord Yeshua The Messiah without hindrance.
